text:
The Mandatory Nap Act of 2026 should be federal law immediately.

Here is the proposal: every person in the United States must take a 20-minute nap between 2:00 PM and 3:00 PM, Monday through Friday. Enforcement falls to the newly created Bureau of Afternoon Rest, which will employ 12,000 inspectors armed with noise meters and fleece blankets.

The case for this is overwhelming.

First, productivity. Studies consistently show that short afternoon naps improve cognitive performance by 34 percent. We currently lose this benefit because people sit at their desks pretending to work while actually staring at email they already read twice. The Mandatory Nap Act converts that wasted time into genuine restoration. Net output goes up.

Second, the economy. A rested workforce makes fewer errors. Fewer errors mean fewer recalls, fewer lawsuits, fewer bridge collapses. The Congressional Budget Office will confirm, once asked, that bridge collapses are expensive. The Mandatory Nap Act pays for itself in reduced infrastructure disaster costs alone.

Third, social cohesion. When everyone naps at the same time, no one has an unfair alertness advantage over anyone else. Currently the wealthy can afford ergonomic chairs and afternoon espresso while working-class people suffer through the 2 PM cortisol crash in plastic seats. The nap equalizes this. Democracy requires shared fatigue management.

Objections are weak. Surgeons can be exempted during active procedures. Pilots are already regulated more heavily than this and survive fine. People who claim they cannot nap will be assigned a dark room and told to at least lie quietly, which research shows provides 60 percent of the cognitive benefit anyway.

The Mandatory Nap Act is humane, fiscally responsible, constitutionally defensible, and long overdue. Vote yes. Then rest.
